unit.name: Segregated Waste Collection & Containment System
unit.cfj: Segregate and contain hazardous, biological, chemical, and sharps waste streams at point of generation

stakeholders.title: Cleanroom Sanitation / Decontamination Technician
stakeholders.cfj_for_stakeholder: Collect, segregate, and contain biological and chemical residue waste streams generated during cleanroom cleaning and changeover at point of generation.

stakeholders.title: Sterile Manufacturing Operator (Fill-Finish Line Operator)
stakeholders.cfj_for_stakeholder: Segregate and contain hazardous, biological, chemical, and sharps waste streams at the point of generation during fill-finish operations on the line.

stakeholders.title: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
stakeholders.cfj_for_stakeholder: Govern that hazardous, chemical, and sharps waste segregation and containment at point of generation meets occupational-safety and regulatory containment limits.

stakeholders.title: Production / Aseptic Operations Supervisor
stakeholders.cfj_for_stakeholder: Ensure waste streams are correctly segregated and contained at point of generation across all shifts without compromising aseptic flow.

rows.stmt: Prevent the number of sterility breaches in the surrounding environment attributable to waste collection tasks
rows.imp_rat: A sterility breach in the surrounding environment from waste tasks is the worst outcome in fill-finish, jeopardizing product and the operator's core mandate.
rows.sat_rat: Strict aseptic procedures generally prevent breaches, though waste activity remains a recognized residual risk.

rows.stmt: Diminish the frequency of false confirmations that a stream is segregated when it has actually been mixed
rows.imp_rat: A false confirmation that streams are segregated when mixed defeats the operator's core segregation job and can create dangerous cross-contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Verification is largely visual/manual with no reliable detection of a mix that already occurred, leaving false positives likely.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a compromised protective barrier going undetected before handling waste
rows.imp_rat: An undetected compromised barrier before handling hazardous waste exposes the operator directly, making this a top personal-safety outcome.
rows.sat_rat: Detection relies on the operator's own visual glove/gown inspection, which readily misses pinholes or micro-tears.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of waste-stream cross-contamination reaching product-contact zones during a fill-finish run
rows.imp_rat: Waste-stream cross-contamination reaching product-contact zones is a catastrophic sterility failure the supervisor exists to prevent.
rows.sat_rat: Barrier segregation, dedicated waste paths and unidirectional flow give reasonable protection, though not absolute assurance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of waste residue or particulate escaping into the cleanroom during collection activity
rows.imp_rat: Residue or particulate escaping during collection directly threatens cleanroom sterility, which is paramount in fill-finish.
rows.sat_rat: Closed containers and cleanroom protocols help, but the act of opening and bagging still generates escape risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of particulate or microbial disturbance introduced into the controlled environment during waste collection
rows.imp_rat: Introducing particulate or microbial disturbance during waste collection directly undermines the classified environment the technician is charged with protecting, making this a core stake.
rows.sat_rat: Current bagging and cart handling inherently generate some particle shedding and turbulence, so disturbance is only partially controlled by existing tools.

rows.stmt: Reduce the likelihood of cross-contamination from waste handling onto cleaned cleanroom surfaces
rows.imp_rat: Cross-contamination from waste handling onto freshly cleaned surfaces directly negates the technician's decontamination work and endangers product sterility.
rows.sat_rat: Sequencing and dedicated paths help, but waste transport through cleaned zones still risks recontamination with today's methods.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of cleanroom contamination spread before a spill is contained
rows.imp_rat: A spill spreading in a sterile fill-finish cleanroom directly threatens product sterility and operator safety, so limiting spread before containment is central to this technician's job.
rows.sat_rat: Spill kits and SOPs exist but response is manual and reactive, so residual spread commonly occurs before full containment today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of undetected protection gaps exposing the operator to residue during collection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected protection gaps expose the technician to biological and chemical residue during collection, making detection of PPE integrity a top personal-safety stake.
rows.sat_rat: PPE donning protocols exist but gaps that develop during the task typically go undetected until symptoms or visible breach appear, leaving this weakly addressed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that waste containment activities introduce particulates or microbes into the aseptic zone during operations
rows.imp_rat: Waste handling that sheds particulates or microbes directly attacks the Grade A/B environment the supervisor must protect.
rows.sat_rat: Sealed bags and closed containers help, but manual manipulation near the zone still generates particulate and microbial risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the number of containment breaches that compromise aseptic-environment integrity during a shift
rows.imp_rat: A containment breach compromising aseptic integrity on shift is a top-severity event squarely within the supervisor's job.
rows.sat_rat: Physical containment and monitoring catch many breaches, but in-process breaches during transfer remain a real gap.

rows.stmt: Prevent the number of skin or residue exposure incidents during disposal tasks at the line
rows.imp_rat: Preventing skin or residue exposure during disposal is a direct personal-health imperative for the operator handling hazardous waste.
rows.sat_rat: Robust PPE and gowning protocols make exposure incidents relatively rare but not eliminated at the line.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residue that escapes containment during the collection task
rows.imp_rat: Residue escaping containment during the collection task is the primary failure mode the technician exists to prevent in a sterile environment.
rows.sat_rat: Established containment consumables capture most residue, but transfer moments and open collection steps still permit escape today.

rows.stmt: Prevent the degree of residue contact reaching skin or airways throughout the containment task
rows.imp_rat: Preventing residue reaching skin or airways throughout the task is the essence of the technician's personal protection during hazardous waste handling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard gowning, gloves and respiratory protection block most contact, but airway and skin exposure risk persists during breaches or splashes.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that personnel exposure to hazardous waste exceeds acceptable limits at the point of generation
rows.imp_rat: Keeping personnel exposure below acceptable limits at point of generation is the literal statutory core of the industrial hygiene manager's job.
rows.sat_rat: Engineering controls, monitoring and PPE keep most exposures within limits, but real-time point-of-generation assurance is imperfect.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the frequency of unprotected contact with hazardous waste during routine handling
rows.imp_rat: Unprotected contact with hazardous waste during routine handling is a core occupational-exposure event the industrial hygiene manager exists to prevent.
rows.sat_rat: PPE, SOPs and containers handle routine handling reasonably well, but lapses in glove/handling discipline still cause contact events.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a misrouted waste item going unnoticed when verifying segregation during a fill-finish run
rows.imp_rat: A misrouted item slipping past verification breaks segregation integrity that the operator is specifically tasked to confirm during the run.
rows.sat_rat: Verification is manual and visual, so a single misrouted item easily goes unnoticed amid line activity.

rows.stmt: Diminish the amount of operator attention diverted from line tasks to handle waste during fill-finish operations
rows.imp_rat: Waste handling steals focus from precise aseptic fill tasks, and any diverted attention risks both product quality and line rhythm.
rows.sat_rat: Typical open bins and manual bagging force the operator to repeatedly stop line tasks to manage waste today.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time taken to detect a leak or breached containment at the fill-finish line
rows.imp_rat: Early detection of a leak or breach limits contamination spread, central to the operator's spill-response duty.
rows.sat_rat: Detection depends on the operator visually noticing a breach, so leaks can persist unseen for a time today.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time taken to detect a leak or breached containment after it occurs
rows.imp_rat: The faster a leak or breach is detected, the less contamination spreads, making rapid detection a high-value part of the technician's containment responsibility.
rows.sat_rat: Detection today relies mostly on visual observation, so leaks are often noticed only after material has already escaped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the frequency of mis-segregated waste streams escaping detection during a shift
rows.imp_rat: An undetected mis-segregated stream can defeat the entire point-of-generation control the supervisor guarantees, threatening compliance.
rows.sat_rat: Detection today relies on visual operator checks and periodic audits, which routinely miss mis-sorts between rounds.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the time between an exposure-limit breach occurring and it being recognized
rows.imp_rat: The lag between an exposure-limit breach and its recognition directly determines how many people are harmed before intervention, a key exposure-governance metric.
rows.sat_rat: Many exposures are detected via periodic sampling or symptoms rather than real-time alarms, so recognition delay is often substantial.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incorrectly segregated waste stream goes undetected during verification
rows.imp_rat: An incorrectly segregated stream slipping through verification defeats the entire control system and can reach disposal undetected, a critical governance gap.
rows.sat_rat: Verification is largely manual visual inspection with sampling gaps, so undetected mis-segregation remains a chronic weakness.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of fill-finish line pauses caused by waste disposal activity during a production run
rows.imp_rat: Line pauses for waste disposal directly cut into fill-finish throughput the operator is measured on.
rows.sat_rat: Current workflows minimize but do not eliminate stops when containers fill or swaps are needed mid-run.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of waste spread before a spill is contained at the line
rows.imp_rat: Limiting how far a spill spreads before containment directly protects the cleanroom and the operator handling hazardous streams.
rows.sat_rat: Spill kits and trays exist but response depends on operator noticing quickly, so spread is only partly limited today.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of waste items placed into the wrong stream at the point of generation
rows.imp_rat: Placing items in the wrong stream at generation is the core sorting failure this operator exists to prevent.
rows.sat_rat: Color-coded, labeled bins guide sorting but human error at the point of generation persists during busy runs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of staged waste leaking or releasing residue while awaiting transfer out of the area
rows.imp_rat: Leaking staged waste releases hazardous residue into the controlled area, a direct failure of the operator's containment duty.
rows.sat_rat: Sealed bags and rigid sharps containers reduce leaks but liquid and residue seepage during staging still happens.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of mislabeled waste streams handed off for transfer out of the production area
rows.imp_rat: Mislabeled streams handed off create downstream disposal and compliance failures the operator is accountable for.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized labels reduce errors but manual label application under time pressure still yields mislabels.

rows.stmt: Diminish the frequency of waste identity records that fail to match the actual contents at handoff
rows.imp_rat: Records that misstate contents at handoff undermine downstream hazardous-waste routing and traceability the operator is accountable for.
rows.sat_rat: Manual labeling and logs mostly work but transcription and swap errors persist under production pressure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a sterility compromise traced to the operator's waste handling in front of quality leadership
rows.imp_rat: Being blamed for a sterility compromise in front of quality leadership is a high-stakes reputational threat central to the operator's standing.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures reduce traceable failures, but the operator has limited proof to defend their handling if questioned.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of compromised personal protection going unconfirmed before the collection task begins
rows.imp_rat: Confirming personal protection is intact before starting the task is a critical safety gate for the technician handling hazardous residue.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-task PPE checks are procedural and self-verified, so compromised protection can go unconfirmed under routine time pressure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the number of point-of-generation containment lapses that go unverified during a shift
rows.imp_rat: Unverified point-of-generation lapses defeat the core promise of containment at source that defines this supervisor's job.
rows.sat_rat: Verification is spot-check and log-based, so lapses between checks routinely go unconfirmed until later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty about whether contamination-risk limits are being met across all shifts
rows.imp_rat: The supervisor must assure contamination-risk limits are met across all shifts, so cross-shift uncertainty is a core exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Environmental monitoring and shift logs give lagging, sampled data rather than continuous cross-shift assurance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a containment seal failing while residue accumulates at the point of generation
rows.imp_rat: A seal failing while residue accumulates at the point of generation risks direct release of hazardous material into the cleanroom, which is squarely the technician's containment responsibility.
rows.sat_rat: Modern bag ties, gaskets and rigid containers are fairly reliable, but seal integrity under accumulating residue is not guaranteed by current equipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the rate of residue placed into the wrong waste stream at the point of generation
rows.imp_rat: Placing residue into the wrong stream at the point of generation defeats the segregation duty at its core and creates commingling and compliance problems downstream.
rows.sat_rat: Color-coded and labeled receptacles guide correct sorting, but time pressure and ambiguous residues still drive occasional misplacement today.

rows.stmt: Prevent the frequency of biological and chemical residue commingling before segregation is complete
rows.imp_rat: Biological and chemical residues commingling before segregation is complete can create dangerous reactions and disposal violations, making prevention a core segregation stake.
rows.sat_rat: Separate labeled streams reduce commingling, but shared collection moments and transient staging still allow mixing before segregation finishes.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a segregation standard fails to align with current occupational-safety and regulatory containment limits
rows.imp_rat: A segregation standard misaligned with current OSHA/RCRA/EPA limits exposes the whole facility to citation, squarely within the manager's Establish Standards duty.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory tracking and periodic SOP review keep standards mostly current, though lag between rule changes and SOP updates persists.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that waste containment introduces contamination into the fill-finish sterile environment
rows.imp_rat: Waste containment introducing contamination into the sterile fill-finish zone jeopardizes product and the manager's containment-integrity mandate simultaneously.
rows.sat_rat: Sealed containers and airflow separation generally prevent ingress, but transfer and container-change steps remain vulnerable points.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the frequency of containment breaches that compromise aseptic conditions during fill-finish operations
rows.imp_rat: Containment breaches that compromise aseptic conditions directly threaten both product sterility and the manager's regulatory containment mandate in fill-finish.
rows.sat_rat: Established cleanroom containment protocols and closed waste handling manage most breaches, though rare lapses still occur.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the number of occasions a person, product, or sterile process is put at risk under the overseer's watch
rows.imp_rat: Every occasion a person, product, or sterile process is put at risk under their watch is the ultimate scorecard of the containment guardian role.
rows.sat_rat: Layered controls keep serious risk occasions infrequent, but near-misses still occur and are not fully eliminated.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of airflow or pressure disturbance caused by waste movement through the aseptic environment
rows.imp_rat: Moving waste through the aseptic environment can disturb the pressure cascade and unidirectional airflow the supervisor must maintain.
rows.sat_rat: Defined transfer routes and airlocks mitigate disturbance, but cart movement and door cycling still perturb airflow.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of containment reaching full capacity before waste removal can keep pace during a run
rows.imp_rat: Containment hitting capacity mid-run forces the operator into disruptive swaps and risks improvised overflow handling.
rows.sat_rat: Removal cadence often lags peak generation, so operators regularly face full containers before pickup.

rows.stmt: Diminish the degree of variability between waste generation rate and available containment capacity at the line
rows.imp_rat: Mismatched capacity versus generation rate forces the operator into unplanned container swaps that interrupt the core containment job.
rows.sat_rat: Fixed-size containers rarely match variable run output, so overflow or premature sealing is common today.

rows.stmt: Diminish the amount of decontamination effort required after a containment breach occurs
rows.imp_rat: After a containment breach the operator must halt line work to decontaminate, so reducing that effort directly protects fill-finish uptime and safety.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on manual wipe-down SOPs and consumables that make post-breach decon slow and labor-intensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty about whether containment was correct at each point of generation
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about correct containment at each generation point directly undermines the supervisor's ability to assure segregation.
rows.sat_rat: Confidence rests on paper logs and periodic walkthroughs, leaving real-time certainty low.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation in how segregation requirements are interpreted across personnel and shifts
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent interpretation across shifts is a primary source of segregation error and undermines the uniformity the manager must enforce.
rows.sat_rat: Training and labeling reduce but never eliminate shift-to-shift human interpretation variance, which stays chronically hard to control.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt that no personnel were exposed beyond acceptable limits on this shift
rows.imp_rat: The manager carries personal accountability for worker safety and needs genuine reassurance that no one was over-exposed on a shift.
rows.sat_rat: Without continuous personal monitoring, doubt lingers until lagging sample results return, leaving residual uncertainty each shift.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a containment or segregation failure on my watch that damages my standing as guardian of aseptic integrity
rows.imp_rat: A containment or segregation failure on their watch is the core threat to this supervisor's standing as guardian of integrity.
rows.sat_rat: Established controls make outright failures uncommon, but the residual risk still weighs on their standing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the frequency of product-safety incidents linked to waste containment that undermine my reputation for protecting aseptic flow
rows.imp_rat: A product-safety incident traced to waste containment directly damages the supervisor's reputation as protector of aseptic flow.
rows.sat_rat: Such incidents are rare under current controls, so reputational hits are infrequent though severe when they occur.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of airflow disturbance introduced by waste handling near the fill-finish line
rows.imp_rat: Airflow disturbance near the line can compromise unidirectional flow protecting exposed product, so the operator must handle waste without disrupting it.
rows.sat_rat: Placement rules and slow movement mitigate disturbance, but any bin opening or transport still perturbs local airflow.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an operator being uncertain which stream a given waste item belongs to during sorting
rows.imp_rat: Operator uncertainty at the sort point is the root cause of misrouting, directly threatening the core segregation job.
rows.sat_rat: Labeled color-coded receptacles and SOPs help, but ambiguous mixed-hazard items still cause hesitation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to direct each waste item into its designated stream during fill-finish operations
rows.imp_rat: Directing each item to its stream quickly during live fill operations keeps segregation from becoming a line bottleneck.
rows.sat_rat: Nearby labeled bins make routing reasonably fast, but reach and decision time still add friction mid-run.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a staged waste stream becoming unidentifiable before transfer out of the area
rows.imp_rat: If staged waste loses its identity before transfer, the operator's segregation and tracking work is nullified at handoff.
rows.sat_rat: Labels and color-coded bags usually hold up but can smear or detach in the staging period.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of segregation lapses that erode the line team's trust in the operator's containment discipline
rows.imp_rat: Segregation lapses that erode team trust threaten the operator's identity as a reliable guardian of containment discipline.
rows.sat_rat: Peer confidence rests on visible discipline, but occasional lapses under current methods still occur and are noticed.

rows.stmt: Prevent the frequency of containment breaches caused by overfilling before handoff
rows.imp_rat: Overfilling that breaches containment before handoff directly releases waste and re-contaminates the space, a failure the technician is responsible for preventing.
rows.sat_rat: Fill-line markings help, but overfilling under time pressure during changeover remains a routine cause of breaches today.

rows.stmt: Prevent the frequency of airflow or pressure disruption caused by waste containment activity in the cleanroom
rows.imp_rat: Waste containment activity that disrupts airflow or pressure cascades can compromise the sterile zone the technician must preserve, so avoiding it matters to cleanroom integrity.
rows.sat_rat: Movement of carts, bags and containers inevitably perturbs unidirectional airflow, and current workflows only loosely mitigate this.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time taken to restore waste containment after a disruption so operations can resume
rows.imp_rat: Faster restoration of containment after disruption lets the supervisor resume aseptic operations without prolonged risk exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery depends on manual cleanup and reverification, which is workable but slow.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the frequency of the same segregation failure recurring after it has been resolved
rows.imp_rat: Recurrence of a resolved segregation failure signals ineffective CAPA and is a governance failure the EHS manager is directly accountable for.
rows.sat_rat: Root-cause and CAPA systems exist but recurrence is common because fixes often rely on human behavior rather than engineered controls.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of generation points that are never checked for segregation adherence
rows.imp_rat: Generation points never checked are blind spots where undetected mis-segregation accumulates, directly weakening verification coverage the manager owns.
rows.sat_rat: Audit rounds cover major points but full coverage of every generation point is rarely achieved with manual inspection resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty about whether waste is correctly segregated and contained at the point of generation right now
rows.imp_rat: Knowing waste is correctly contained at generation right now is precisely the reassurance this supervisor seeks in their job.
rows.sat_rat: Point-in-time certainty is unavailable between manual checks, leaving persistent live uncertainty.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty about whether every waste stream is correctly segregated right now
rows.imp_rat: Real-time certainty that every stream is correctly segregated right now is emotionally desired but hard to demand given point-in-time controls.
rows.sat_rat: Current tools give no live, facility-wide segregation status, so the manager operates with substantial standing uncertainty.

rows.stmt: Minimize the frequency of recurring containment disruptions from the same unresolved root cause
rows.imp_rat: Repeat disruptions from the same root cause consume the supervisor's shift and signal an unclosed CAPA loop under their remit.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation/CAPA systems exist but recurrence is common when fixes are procedural rather than engineered out.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between waste generation and confirmation that it is correctly contained at its source
rows.imp_rat: A long gap between generation and containment confirmation widens the window for aseptic compromise the supervisor owns.
rows.sat_rat: Confirmation typically waits for the next round or log entry, so lag is inherent in current practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the frequency of unexpected containment surprises that undermine confidence the aseptic flow is intact
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected containment surprises shake the supervisor's felt confidence that aseptic flow is intact, which drives their vigilance.
rows.sat_rat: With mostly lagging indicators, surprises still emerge from audits and EM excursions rather than being pre-empted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency in containment performance across shifts that erodes trust in me as the cross-shift guardian
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent containment performance across shifts undermines trust in the supervisor as the cross-shift guardian.
rows.sat_rat: Shift handovers and varying operator diligence make cross-shift consistency hard to demonstrate with current tools.

rows.stmt: Reduce the likelihood of uncertainty about which stream a given residue belongs to during collection
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about which stream a residue belongs to during collection slows the technician and drives segregation errors, so clarity has solid job value.
rows.sat_rat: Labeling and SOP charts resolve most cases, but novel or mixed residues still leave the technician guessing at the point of collection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a contained waste stream being mislabeled before handoff
rows.imp_rat: Mislabeling a contained waste stream before handoff creates downstream compliance and disposal errors the technician is accountable for, so accurate identity matters substantially.
rows.sat_rat: Color-coded bags and printed labels handle most cases, though manual labeling still leaves room for occasional mislabels today.

rows.stmt: Prevent the rate of identity mismatch between a container and its actual contents at handoff
rows.imp_rat: Identity mismatch between a container and its actual contents at handoff propagates hazardous disposal errors the technician is accountable for at the transfer point.
rows.sat_rat: Verification at handoff catches many mismatches, but reliance on manual checks means some containers still transfer with wrong identity.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the number of waste streams whose segregation requirements are undefined at the point of generation
rows.imp_rat: Undefined segregation requirements for a waste stream leave handlers guessing at the point of generation, a foundational Establish-Standards failure.
rows.sat_rat: Waste characterization programs cover most known streams, but novel or process-change streams periodically arrive undefined.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a containment failure that publicly discredits the overseer's standing as guardian of source containment
rows.imp_rat: A publicly discrediting containment failure threatens the manager's professional standing as the recognized guardian of source containment.
rows.sat_rat: Major public failures are relatively rare under current programs, so reputational standing is generally preserved though never guaranteed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of nagging doubt about whether any stream was cross-contaminated on the operator's watch
rows.imp_rat: Nagging doubt about cross-contamination on their watch is a real emotional burden tied to the operator's accountability.
rows.sat_rat: With no confirmatory feedback that mixing didn't occur, operators carry lingering uncertainty today.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of moments when the operator cannot feel sure every stream stayed correctly segregated
rows.imp_rat: Frequent moments of not feeling sure about segregation reflect a gap in reassurance that affects the operator's peace of mind.
rows.sat_rat: Current manual verification gives little ongoing assurance, so uncertainty recurs across a run.

rows.stmt: Prevent the likelihood of anxiety from unseen leaks compromising the operator's sense of control
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety from unseen leaks undermines the technician's sense of control over containment, a real emotional cost in this high-stakes role.
rows.sat_rat: Because leaks are hard to see until they spread, the status quo does little to relieve worry about hidden breaches.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the time the overseer waits for assurance that current segregation is correct at the point of generation
rows.imp_rat: Reducing the wait for assurance that segregation is correct supports the manager's need to feel in control, though it is secondary to actual safety outcomes.
rows.sat_rat: Assurance arrives only after scheduled audits, leaving noticeable waiting gaps in confidence.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the frequency of surprise segregation lapses that undermine confidence between checks
rows.imp_rat: Surprise lapses between checks erode the manager's confidence in the control system, an emotional need tied to sustained vigilance.
rows.sat_rat: Periodic checks leave long unmonitored intervals, so surprise lapses between audits remain common and unsettling.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the time before the overseer can confirm that no exposure event occurred on the shift
rows.imp_rat: Sooner confirmation that no exposure event occurred lets the manager close out a shift with confidence, an emotional-reassurance need.
rows.sat_rat: Confirmation typically waits on delayed monitoring data or incident reports rather than shift-end assurance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of re-cleaning and re-validation required after a containment disruption is resolved
rows.imp_rat: Re-cleaning and re-validation after a disruption costs the supervisor line time and documentation burden, but is a downstream consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery follows lengthy standard requalification protocols with little means to reduce the scope required.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt about whether waste containment on the current shift is holding the aseptic boundary
rows.imp_rat: Lingering doubt about whether the boundary is holding on the current shift is a real emotional load for the accountable supervisor.
rows.sat_rat: Real-time reassurance is thin; the supervisor relies on absence of alarms rather than positive confirmation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time needed to confirm that each waste stream remains correctly segregated at the line
rows.imp_rat: Faster segregation confirmation frees the operator for line tasks, though the confirmation itself matters more than its speed.
rows.sat_rat: Manual visual checks are moderately quick but scale poorly across multiple streams during a run.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in the time required to dispose of waste between operating cycles
rows.imp_rat: Unpredictable disposal time between cycles disrupts the operator's ability to plan line pacing, but it is a throughput annoyance more than a hazard.
rows.sat_rat: Disposal timing today swings with container fill state and waste type, giving moderate but inconsistent performance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time needed to ready collected waste for transfer out of the production area
rows.imp_rat: Reducing prep time for transfer helps the operator keep the area clear without stealing time from fill tasks.
rows.sat_rat: Sealing, labeling and documenting waste for transfer is a moderately time-consuming manual routine today.

rows.stmt: Diminish the amount of production floor space occupied by waste staged for removal
rows.imp_rat: Cleanroom floor space is tightly constrained, so waste staged for removal competing with the line footprint matters but is not safety-critical.
rows.sat_rat: Current staging carts and designated zones partially contain footprint but bins still accumulate during a run.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to confirm protection is intact before each disposal task
rows.imp_rat: Confirming intact protection quickly matters for safety cadence, but the confirmation's reliability outweighs its speed.
rows.sat_rat: A quick visual PPE self-check is fast but crude, giving moderate perceived performance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt about whether protection is fully intact while handling hazardous waste streams
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about barrier integrity weighs on the operator handling hazardous streams and affects their confidence to work efficiently.
rows.sat_rat: Reliance on self-inspection leaves residual doubt that current tools do not fully resolve.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of moments when the operator feels uncertain they have avoided contact with waste residue
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about having avoided residue contact is an emotional safety concern for the operator during disposal.
rows.sat_rat: PPE gives moderate reassurance, but operators still second-guess whether contact occurred.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time collection is interrupted when available containment capacity is exhausted during changeover
rows.imp_rat: Interruptions when capacity is exhausted during changeover stall the technician and delay line turnover, giving moderate importance to reducing that downtime.
rows.sat_rat: Swapping full containers mid-task causes recurring stoppages that current logistics only partially buffer.

rows.stmt: Prevent the likelihood of generation outpacing collection capacity during peak cleaning periods
rows.imp_rat: When generation outpaces collection capacity during peak cleaning, the technician is forced into unsafe workarounds, so capacity keeping up matters moderately to the job.
rows.sat_rat: Container fleets are sized for typical loads but peak changeover surges regularly strain available capacity in current setups.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of re-cleaning required after a containment breach during changeover
rows.imp_rat: Re-cleaning after a containment breach during changeover consumes the technician's time and delays line release, so minimizing it has meaningful job value.
rows.sat_rat: When breaches occur, current practice requires full re-clean of affected zones with little to reduce the burden.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of fear of hazardous residue exposure felt while collecting and containing waste
rows.imp_rat: Fear of hazardous residue exposure while collecting waste is a genuine felt burden that affects the technician's comfort and focus on the job.
rows.sat_rat: Trusted PPE reduces fear somewhat, but handling unknown hazardous residues still leaves an underlying apprehension today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of uncertainty about whether protection is adequate undermining the operator's sense of safety
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about whether protection is adequate erodes the technician's sense of safety and can distract from careful execution, making this emotionally significant.
rows.sat_rat: PPE protocols offer baseline confidence, but the lack of real-time protection verification leaves a nagging uncertainty during hazardous handling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of operator time diverted from production to handle waste containment during a shift
rows.imp_rat: Diverting operators to waste handling erodes fill-finish throughput the supervisor is accountable for, but it ranks below contamination stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Current manual bagging and cart-out routines still pull line operators away mid-run, though scheduled waste rounds partially contain the loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the frequency of containment capacity being exceeded by waste generation during peak production periods
rows.imp_rat: When containment bins fill during peak batching the supervisor must intervene to keep segregation intact, so capacity headroom matters.
rows.sat_rat: Fixed-size containers sized to average load are routinely overwhelmed at peak, forcing improvised overflow handling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time waste accumulates at point of generation while awaiting available containment capacity
rows.imp_rat: Waste accumulating at source while awaiting capacity creates the very containment risk the supervisor must prevent at generation points.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled removal usually keeps pace, but accumulation still occurs when capacity lags peak generation.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of cross-contaminated waste that must be re-segregated after a separation failure
rows.imp_rat: Re-segregating cross-contaminated waste creates added handling and exposure events, but it is a downstream consequence rather than the primary safety breach the manager governs.
rows.sat_rat: Status-quo relies on manual sorting corrections that reliably fix but do not prevent the rework volume, leaving mediocre performance.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the time to confirm that waste streams are correctly segregated at the point of generation
rows.imp_rat: Faster confirmation of correct segregation improves throughput of oversight, but timeliness matters less than certainty for this safety role.
rows.sat_rat: Manual walk-through verification is slow and periodic, giving only intermittent rather than prompt confirmation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for confirmation that point-of-generation containment is verified
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for verification confirmation delays the supervisor's peace of mind but is a lesser stake than the containment fact itself.
rows.sat_rat: Confirmation is tied to round schedules and paperwork, so waiting time is routinely long.

rows.stmt: Reduce the variability between waste generation rate and available containment capacity across shifts
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between generation rate and capacity across shifts complicates the supervisor's cross-shift consistency but is a planning issue.
rows.sat_rat: Static capacity provisioning poorly tracks shift-to-shift variability in production load.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of waste identity becoming unreadable or ambiguous between collection and handoff
rows.imp_rat: Waste identity becoming unreadable between collection and handoff creates ambiguity that risks mishandling, so legibility matters moderately to the tracking job.
rows.sat_rat: Labels usually survive the short transit, though residue, moisture and abrasion occasionally render them ambiguous in current practice.

rows.stmt: Prevent the likelihood of a contamination event traced to the operator undermining their reputation as a dependable guardian
rows.imp_rat: A contamination event traced back to the technician directly threatens their reputation as a dependable contamination-control guardian, giving this real status weight.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability systems and shared responsibility usually diffuse blame, but a clearly attributed event still damages standing under the status quo.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the time to correct a waste stream found incorrectly separated after the failure is identified
rows.imp_rat: Quick correction after a mis-separation is identified limits exposure duration, but the greater stakes lie in detection rather than correction speed.
rows.sat_rat: Once flagged, staff can usually re-sort promptly, so correction time is handled adequately by current workflows.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which waste handling disrupts the controlled airflow of the sterile zone
rows.imp_rat: Waste handling that disrupts controlled airflow is a real aseptic risk but is more the domain of facilities/QA than the EHS manager's exposure focus.
rows.sat_rat: Airflow-aware SOPs and container placement usually preserve laminar flow, though ad-hoc handling can locally disturb it.

rows.stk: Environmental Health & Safety (EHS) / Industrial Hygiene Manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of incidents that cause others to question whether hazardous waste is always contained at the source
rows.imp_rat: Incidents that make others doubt source containment erode the trust the manager depends on to govern effectively, a status concern.
rows.sat_rat: Documented programs and audit trails usually sustain stakeholder confidence, though occasional incidents still prompt scrutiny.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time production is paused to accommodate waste segregation tasks during a run
rows.imp_rat: Pausing a fill-finish run for segregation tasks hurts throughput but is usually a brief, manageable interruption.
rows.sat_rat: Segregation is largely designed into workflow, so pauses are occasional rather than chronic.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of available containment volume left unused when a container is sealed for removal
rows.imp_rat: Unused container volume is a cost/efficiency concern for the operator's staging but carries little safety or quality weight.
rows.sat_rat: Sealing often occurs on schedule or hazard rules rather than fill level, leaving volume routinely wasted today.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt that every waste stream remains securely contained on the operator's watch
rows.imp_rat: Reducing doubt that every stream stays contained on their watch matters to the technician's peace of mind but is a secondary emotional layer over the functional task.
rows.sat_rat: Visible intact containers give partial reassurance, but no continuous confirmation leaves some residual doubt through the shift.

rows.stmt: Minimize the frequency of uncertainty about whether segregation was done correctly during the shift
rows.imp_rat: Lingering uncertainty about whether segregation was done correctly weighs on the technician across the shift, giving this emotional outcome moderate relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists provide some reassurance, but without confirmation feedback the technician often carries residual doubt about earlier sorting.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a visible containment failure damaging the operator's standing with quality leadership
rows.imp_rat: A visible containment failure seen by quality leadership can damage the technician's standing, so avoiding such exposure has moderate status value.
rows.sat_rat: Failures are relatively infrequent under current controls, so most technicians maintain reasonable standing, though a single visible lapse can hurt.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of inconsistent containment outcomes eroding the cleanroom team's trust in the operator
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent containment outcomes erode the cleanroom team's trust in the technician, which matters to their standing but sits below core safety stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized SOPs promote fairly consistent results, so team trust is generally maintained though variability occasionally undercuts it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of unused containment volume left when a container is removed from service
rows.imp_rat: Maximizing the volume used before a waste container leaves service affects changeover efficiency and consumable cost, but it is secondary to the containment and contamination stakes that define the technician's job.
rows.sat_rat: Status-quo protocols usually pull containers at fill-line or time triggers, leaving some unused headroom but generally within acceptable practice today.
